title: iTransformer: Inverted Transformers Are Effective for Time Series Forecasting
authors: Yong Liu, Tengge Hu, Haoran Zhang, Haixu Wu, Shiyu Wang, Lintao Ma, Mingsheng Long (Tsinghua University; Ant Group)
tokenization_method: VARIATE-AS-TOKEN via dimension INVERSION. Vanilla Transformers for forecasting embed one TIMESTAMP (the vector of all variates at time t) into one token, so attention runs over the time axis and each token mixes heterogeneous variates. iTransformer INVERTS this: the WHOLE observed lookback series of a single variate x^(i) in R^T (T = lookback length) is embedded by a shared MLP into ONE D-dim variate token h^(i) = MLP(x^(i)). With N variates you get N tokens. Self-attention then runs over the N variate tokens (modeling MULTIVARIATE CORRELATIONS), LayerNorm is applied per variate token (normalizing each series' representation, which the paper argues tackles non-stationarity / inter-variate distribution gaps), and a shared position-wise FFN processes each variate token independently to learn nonlinear series representations / implicit temporal patterns. A linear projection head maps each variate token back to its S-step forecast. No changes to the core Transformer block components are needed — only the embedding/projection are inverted; hence 'Inverted Transformers'.
feature_types_handled: numerical / temporal ONLY. Input is a multivariate continuous time series: N numeric variates each observed over T timesteps. No categorical pathway, no high-cardinality fields, no explicit missing-value handling, no static-covariate pathway. Each variate token summarizes one real-valued series. Variates may be heterogeneous in physical meaning/units (e.g. different sensors), and the per-token LayerNorm is the mechanism intended to reconcile that heterogeneity — but they are all still NUMERIC time series, not mixed numeric+categorical+static.
token_granularity: per-variate (channel-as-token): ONE token = one entire variate's lookback series (all T timesteps of that variate collapsed into a single D-dim embedding by an MLP). NOT per-timestep (vanilla), NOT a temporal patch (PatchTST), NOT per-value, NOT field-value. Sequence length seen by attention = N = number of variates, INDEPENDENT of lookback length T (T only changes the MLP input width).
numerical_strategy: Whole-series MLP embedding: the T raw (post-RevIN-style normalization) scalar values of a variate are fed as a flat T-vector into a shared MLP that outputs the D-dim token; temporal structure is encoded implicitly inside this MLP and later refined by the FFN. No piecewise-linear, no periodic/Fourier, no binning, no per-scalar embedding. This is a coarse, learned, global-per-series encoder — richness comes from a whole-series view + nonlinear FFN, not from a clever per-value embedding. Series-level reversible instance normalization (subtract mean / divide std per variate, restored at output) handles distribution shift; LayerNorm across variate tokens further reduces inter-variate distribution divergence.
temporal_handling: Temporal dynamics are pushed ENTIRELY INSIDE the per-variate token: the MLP embedding + the shared FFN are responsible for capturing temporal patterns of each series. The Transformer's attention does NOT model time at all — it models inter-variate correlation. There is no explicit temporal position encoding (the whole series is one token), no recurrence, no patching. Consequence: the model relies on the MLP/FFN to learn temporal representation and on attention to mix variates. This is the inverse of PatchTST (attention over time, channel-independent).
scales_to_many_features: Directly relevant — but with a real cost caveat. Attention is now O(N^2) in the NUMBER OF VARIATES N (not in time), so a wide feature set drives the quadratic term. Demonstrated on Traffic (N=862 variates) and PEMS (up to 883) at SOTA accuracy, which is concrete evidence the variate-as-token scheme works at the 70+-and-far-beyond feature count this topic targets. To make wide-N affordable the paper proposes EFFICIENT TRAINING: randomly sample a subset of variates per batch (because all variate tokens share the same embedding/FFN/attention weights, the learned mapping generalizes to unsampled variates) — memory drops substantially with performance 'comparable to full-variate training'. So unlike PatchTST's linear-in-M sequential channel-independence, iTransformer puts all variates in ONE joint attention so cross-feature interaction is modeled, at quadratic-in-N cost mitigated by variate subsampling.
downstream_model: Vanilla Transformer ENCODER reused almost verbatim (multi-head self-attention + position-wise FFN + LayerNorm + residuals), only with embedding/head inverted to operate on variate tokens. Typical config: a few encoder layers, D~128-512. The 'iTransformers' framing shows the inversion is a wrapper applicable to ANY attention backbone (Reformer, Informer, Flowformer, FlashAttention), not a new block.
pretraining_objective: none in this paper — fully supervised forecasting (L2/MSE on the horizon). Authors flag large-scale pre-training as future work. The shared-weight variate-token design is what makes transfer plausible (same map applies to any variate), but no SSL pretext is proposed here.
key_results: SOTA on multivariate long-term forecasting across ETTh1/h2/m1/m2, Weather, Electricity(ECL), Traffic, Solar-Energy, and the PEMS datasets (averaged over horizons {96,192,336,720}; PEMS shorter). Reported average MSE (lower better): ECL 0.178 (PatchTST 0.216, TimesNet 0.192, DLinear 0.212, Crossformer 0.244); Traffic 0.428 (PatchTST 0.555, TimesNet 0.620, DLinear 0.625); Solar-Energy 0.233 (PatchTST 0.270, TimesNet 0.301); PEMS avg ~0.119 (PatchTST 0.217, TimesNet 0.148); Weather 0.258 (~tie with PatchTST/TimesNet 0.259); ETTh1/h2 avg 0.383 (PatchTST ~0.381 — essentially a TIE on ETT). Largest wins are on high-dimensional datasets (Traffic 862, ECL 321, Solar, PEMS). vs vanilla Transformer: ECL 0.178 vs 0.277, Traffic 0.428 vs 0.665, Weather 0.258 vs 0.657. FRAMEWORK GENERALITY: simply inverting existing Transformers yields large average MSE promotions — Transformer +38.9%, Reformer +36.1%, Informer +28.5%, FlashAttention +32.2%, Flowformer +16.8%. GENERALIZATION TO UNSEEN VARIATES: training on only ~20% of variates (partition into 5 folds, train on one) and forecasting ALL variates zero-shot retains strong accuracy, beating channel-independent baselines. LOOKBACK: iTransformer accuracy IMPROVES with longer lookback T in {48,96,192,336,720}, unlike vanilla Transformer forecasters which often fail to benefit from longer history.
baselines_compared: PatchTST (2023), Crossformer, TimesNet, DLinear (linear SOTA), and the Transformer family Transformer/Reformer/Informer/Autoformer/FEDformer/Pyraformer/Flowformer; plus RLinear / SCINet referenced. The inverting-framework experiment uses Transformer, Reformer, Informer, Flowformer, FlashAttention as the backbones being wrapped.
transferable_idea: FEATURE-AS-TOKEN over a JOINT attention with SHARED per-token weights. iTransformer is essentially FT-Transformer's 'one token per feature' idea applied to TIME-VARYING features: each feature (variate) becomes a single token via a shared encoder, all feature tokens go into ONE self-attention so cross-feature interaction is learned explicitly, and a per-token FFN learns the feature's own representation. For the 70+ heterogeneous-feature topic this is the cleanest blueprint for the time-varying half AND a unification story: encode each time-varying feature's history (MLP here, or PatchTST patching for finer temporal structure) into one token, encode each static/categorical feature into one token by its own type-appropriate encoder, then run one joint attention over the mixed token set. Two further reusable ideas: (a) per-token LayerNorm/RevIN to reconcile heterogeneous-scale features inside one sequence; (b) random variate-subsampling per batch to keep O(N^2) attention affordable at large feature counts — both directly applicable when N=70+ tokens.
limitations: Authors-stated: only supervised single-task forecasting; large-scale pretraining and other TS tasks left as future work; no explicit failure-mode list. Inferred: (1) attention is O(N^2) in variate count — for very wide feature sets it is slower/heavier than channel-independent models unless the variate-subsampling trick or an efficient attention backbone is used; (2) the whole-series MLP token is a COARSE temporal encoder — it discards fine local temporal structure that patching preserves, so on tasks needing fine temporal resolution a patch-based token may dominate; (3) no temporal position information across the series within a token; (4) results are essentially TIED with PatchTST on low-dimensional ETT datasets — the advantage concentrates on HIGH-dimensional datasets, i.e. the method helps precisely when there are many correlated variates; (5) no categorical/static/missing handling.
pitfalls: 1) DEPTH-OF-EVIDENCE: the variate-as-token win is concentrated on high-N datasets (Traffic/ECL/Solar/PEMS) and is a near-TIE with PatchTST on the small ETT sets — do NOT cite iTransformer as universally beating patch-based tokenization; its edge is specifically 'many correlated variates'. 2) HETEROGENEITY OVERCLAIM: 'variates' here are all NUMERIC time series differing in scale/semantics; LayerNorm reconciling them is NOT the same as fusing numeric+categorical+static tokens. Borrowing the joint-attention-over-feature-tokens pattern is sound; claiming iTransformer 'handles heterogeneous features' in the topic's mixed-type sense is an extrapolation that needs its own validation. 3) The whole-series MLP token is a weak per-feature temporal encoder; for the topic's time-varying half, prefer pairing the joint-variate-attention idea with PatchTST-style patch tokens (or PLR/periodic per-value embeddings) rather than a single flat-MLP-over-T. 4) 'Generalizes to unseen variates / train on 20%' is enabled by SHARED token weights across HOMOGENEOUS-role numeric variates; it will NOT transfer to a held-out CATEGORICAL feature whose token map was never learned. 5) Quadratic-in-N attention: at 70+ features it is fine, but a sweeping 'scales to hundreds of features cheaply' claim ignores that the 862-variate runs lean on the subsampling trick / accept the cost. 6) Gains are partly confounded with RevIN-style normalization and longer lookback, as in much of this literature.
relevance_to_topic: High-value structural anchor for the temporal-feature-tokenization route AND a bridge to tabular-transformers: it shows the FT-Transformer 'feature-as-token + joint self-attention' recipe transfers to time-varying features and that explicit cross-feature attention (vs PatchTST channel-independence) wins exactly when many correlated features coexist — the topic's regime. It supplies the unification template (each feature -> one token by a type-appropriate encoder -> one joint attention) plus two scaling/heterogeneity tools (per-token norm, variate subsampling) for a 70+ mixed feature set.
code_available: yes — official PyTorch repo https://github.com/thuml/iTransformer (well-maintained, integrated into the Time-Series-Library/tslib; configs and scripts for all reported datasets provided).
